title: Chocolate-chip cookies
subtitle: Crisp edges, chewy centres, pools of dark chocolate
excerpt: A reliable chocolate-chip cookie with a crisp rim and a chewy centre. Brown sugar and a 30-minute chill give the dough time to relax, so the cookies spread just enough without going flat.
sourceNotes: Ruth Wakefield, Toll House Tried and True Recipes (1938): the original chocolate chip cookie, developed at the Toll House Inn, Whitman, Massachusetts, in the mid-1930s. The recipe appeared on Nestlé semi-sweet chocolate morsels packaging from 1939 and was republished in American household staples including Joy of Cooking (Irma Rombauer, 1936 and later editions) and The Fannie Farmer Cookbook (various editions from 1896). The technique of chilling shaped cookie dough before baking to control spread is documented in American baking literature from the 1950s onward. Throughout, 'bicarbonate of soda' (British) is used as the equivalent of 'baking soda' (American).

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze unbaked dough balls on a lined tray until solid, then transfer to a freezer bag. Bake from frozen at 180°C fan for 14–15 minutes. Baked cookies freeze well for up to 3 months; defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes.
recipe.batchNotes: The dough doubles well. Use a large bowl and work in two rounds of mixing if necessary.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Shaped dough balls keep in the fridge, covered, for up to 3 days. Bake as needed.

recipeTools.notes: To cover the bowl during chilling.

glossaryTerms.definition: Resting shaped dough in the fridge before baking. Cold fat melts more slowly in the oven, which slows spread and produces a thicker, chewier cookie. The rest also allows the flour to hydrate fully, deepening the flavour.

content.text: Chocolate-chip cookies are the most forgiving recipe in the biscuit tin, which is probably why everyone has a version. This one aims for a crisp edge and a chewy centre: a higher proportion of brown sugar to white, and a 30-minute rest in the fridge before baking. Skip the rest and the cookies spread flat; keep it in and they come out thick, with a slightly underdone centre that firms to the right texture as they cool.

Use dark chocolate with at least 60% cocoa solids, roughly chopped from a bar. Pre-made chips are coated to hold their shape; chopped chocolate melts unevenly, so some pockets are entirely liquid and others remain softly set. That variation is most of what makes a good cookie interesting to eat.
What you need

items.prepNote: for the dough, lightly crushed; a pinch of flakes to finish (optional)
minimum 60% cocoa solids, roughly chopped into uneven chunks

content.text: Weigh the butter and leave it out of the fridge for at least an hour before you start. It should be soft enough to dent easily when pressed but not greasy or shiny. Butter that is too cold will not cream properly; butter that is too warm produces flat cookies.
Method
Mix the dry ingredients
Whisk {{plain-flour}} plain flour, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}} bicarbonate of soda, and 3 g of the crushed salt together in a bowl. Set aside.
Cream the butter and sugars

Beat {{unsalted-butter}} softened butter with {{light-brown-sugar}} light brown sugar and {{caster-sugar}} caster sugar in a large bowl until the mixture is pale and noticeably fluffy, about 3–4 minutes by hand with a wooden spoon or 2 minutes with an electric mixer on medium speed. This stage matters: proper aeration now means the cookie holds a slight lift at the centre rather than baking into a flat disc.
Add the eggs and vanilla

Beat in {{eggs}} the eggs one at a time. Make sure each egg is fully incorporated before adding the next; if the mixture looks curdled, keep beating and it will come together. Add {{vanilla-extract}} vanilla extract and beat through.
Fold in flour and chocolate
Add the flour mixture all at once and fold with a spatula until no streaks of dry flour remain. Fold in {{dark-chocolate}} the chocolate chunks. The dough will be stiff and sticky; that is correct.
Chill the dough
Cover the bowl with cling film and refrigerate for 30 minutes. This is the
dough chilling
step. Cold fat melts slowly in the oven, so the cookies thicken vertically before they spread sideways. A chilled dough also tastes better; the flour has time to hydrate fully.
Shape and bake

Heat the oven to 180°C fan. Scoop the dough into balls of about 45–50 g each and place them on two lined baking trays with at least 7 cm between each cookie — they will spread significantly. Press each ball down lightly so it is not quite round. Scatter a few sea-salt flakes over the top if you like.

Bake one tray at a time on the middle shelf for 11–13 minutes. The cookies are done when the edges are set and deep golden-brown, with no raw-looking line at the rim. The centres will look pale and barely set — this is correct. Remove the tray and do not touch the cookies; they are too fragile to lift while hot.
Cool on the tray

Leave the cookies on the baking tray for 8 minutes after taking them from the oven. As they cool, the centres firm from the outside inward. Transfer to a cooling rack and leave for a further 5 minutes before eating. The final texture, crisp at the edge and yielding in the centre, develops only as they cool.
Troubleshooting

attrs.heading: When things go sideways
attrs.intro: Three common problems and their causes.

items.cause: Butter was too warm or soft, or the dough was not chilled long enough.
items.fix: Butter should be soft but still hold its shape when pressed. If it looks shiny or greasy, it is too warm. Chill the dough for at least 30 minutes; if the kitchen is warm, chill the shaped balls for 20 minutes on the tray before baking.

items.symptom: Cookies come out cakey and puffed rather than chewy
items.cause: Too much flour, typically from scooping the measuring cup into the bag rather than weighing.
items.fix: Weigh all ingredients. Excess flour absorbs the moisture that would otherwise give the cookie chew, and too much bicarbonate of soda causes excessive lift.

items.symptom: Edges are overdone before the centre sets
items.cause: Oven runs hot, or the trays were too close together in the oven.
items.fix: Use an oven thermometer to verify the temperature. Bake one tray at a time on the middle shelf. If your oven has noticeable hot spots, rotate the tray 180° halfway through the bake time.

content.text: A note on the recipe

Ruth Wakefield developed the chocolate chip cookie at the Toll House Inn in Massachusetts in the mid-1930s. The recipe was published in her 1938 book and subsequently printed on Nestlé chocolate packaging from 1939. It spread through American household cookbooks and became the most widely reproduced cookie recipe in the United States. The version here uses the proportions that have proved most consistent across domestic ovens, with dark chocolate in place of semi-sweet chips and a chilling step drawn from mid-century American baking guides.
